moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the queen's pawn, immediately staking a claim in the center and controlling the important e5 and c5 squares. This move allows our dark-squared bishop to develop while keeping our options open for various pawn structures. The d4 opening tends to lead to more positional, strategic games compared to 1.e4, and we're setting up potential pawn advances like e4 or c4 to build a strong central presence. This is the starting move of many classical openings including the Queen's Gambit, King's Indian setups, and various Indian defenses.

Black immediately challenges our central d4 pawn with the knight, following the hypermodern principle of controlling the center with pieces rather than occupying it with pawns. This knight move is the hallmark of the Indian defenses, where Black allows White to build a pawn center while preparing to strike back later. The knight on f6 also prevents our immediate e4 advance and keeps Black's options open for various defensive setups including the King's Indian, Nimzo-Indian, or Queen's Indian depending on how we continue. Black is essentially saying 'go ahead and build your center, I'll attack it when the time is right.'

We're expanding on the queenside and fighting for control of the crucial d5 and b5 squares, while also supporting a potential future e4 advance. This move defines many of the most aggressive systems against the Indian defenses, as we're trying to build the maximum possible pawn center. The c4 advance also opens possibilities for our queen and light-squared bishop while restricting Black's pawn breaks with moves like ...b5. By combining d4 and c4, we're creating what's known as the 'English-style' center that can transpose into various aggressive systems depending on Black's response.

Black commits to the King's Indian Defense setup by preparing to fianchetto the dark-squared bishop on g7, from where it will exert tremendous pressure on our center and the long diagonal. This pawn move is slightly weakening around Black's king, but it's a calculated risk that allows the bishop to become a powerful piece in the coming middlegame battles. The g6 move also prepares ...Bg7 and eventual castling kingside, while keeping the center flexible for potential pawn breaks like ...e5 or ...c5. Black is following the classical King's Indian strategy of allowing White to build a large pawn center while preparing dynamic counterplay.

We're developing our knight to its most natural square, supporting our d4 pawn and preparing for the aggressive Four Pawns Attack with e4. This knight placement also keeps pressure on Black's potential ...d5 break and supports our expanding pawn center. From c3, the knight controls important central squares like e4, d5, and b5, while also opening the possibility of advancing to d5 at the right moment. The knight development is essential for our upcoming e4 push, as it ensures our d4 pawn remains well-protected when we launch our central advance.

Black completes the fianchetto setup by developing the dark-squared bishop to its most active square, where it immediately puts pressure on our central pawns and controls the long diagonal. This bishop will become Black's most important piece in the King's Indian, often supporting tactical shots and pawn breaks throughout the game. The Bg7 also adds extra protection to Black's kingside and prepares for castling, while maintaining flexibility in the center. From g7, this bishop will be perfectly placed to support the typical King's Indian pawn breaks like ...e5 or ...f5, making it a key piece in Black's counterattacking strategy.

We're now establishing the full Four Pawns Attack formation, creating a massive pawn center that controls vast amounts of space and restricts Black's pieces. This aggressive pawn advance gains time by attacking Black's knight and forces it to move, while also opening lines for our light-squared bishop and queen. The Four Pawns Attack is one of White's most ambitious systems against the King's Indian, aiming for a quick kingside attack while maintaining central dominance. However, this pawn center also becomes a target for Black's counterplay, and we must be prepared to defend it while pursuing our attacking chances on the kingside.

Black establishes a solid pawn chain and prepares to challenge our center with moves like ...e5, while also clearing the d8-h4 diagonal for potential tactics. This move supports Black's typical King's Indian strategy of allowing White's pawn center to advance while preparing to strike back with well-timed pawn breaks. The d6 pawn also provides crucial support for a future ...e5 advance and prevents our knight from jumping to e5. By playing ...d6, Black is following classical King's Indian theory, building a flexible position that can explode into tactical complications when the right moment arrives.

We're completing the Four Pawns Attack setup by placing our fourth pawn in the center, creating maximum space advantage and preparing for a potential kingside assault. This move supports a future e5 advance while also preparing to meet Black's ...e5 with f5, gaining space on the kingside and opening attacking chances. The f4 pawn also helps control the crucial e5 and g5 squares, limiting Black's piece activity in the center. However, this aggressive pawn advance also weakens our kingside structure and creates potential targets for Black's counterplay, making accurate play essential in the coming middlegame.

Black wisely gets the king to safety before the position becomes too sharp, while also bringing the rook into play on f8 where it supports the crucial f7 square and potential ...f5 advances. Castling is almost mandatory in the King's Indian Defense, as the positions tend to become very tactical very quickly, and having the king safely tucked away is essential. This move also completes Black's basic development and prepares for the typical King's Indian counterplay with moves like ...e5, ...f5, or ...c5. Black is now ready to launch the characteristic pawn storms that make the King's Indian Defense so dangerous for both sides.

We're developing our kingside knight to its most flexible square, supporting our pawn center while keeping options open for both attack and defense. This knight on f3 controls important central squares like e5 and d4, while also preparing for potential kingside castling to get our own king to safety. The knight placement also supports our e4 pawn and helps prevent Black's ...e5 advance for the moment, giving us time to complete our development. From f3, the knight can later jump to more aggressive squares like g5 or h4 to support a kingside attack, or retreat to defend if Black's counterplay becomes threatening.

Black strikes at our pawn center with the first major challenge, attacking our d4 pawn and creating immediate tension in the position. This pawn break is one of the most common and effective ways for Black to fight back in the King's Indian, as it opens lines for the pieces and creates tactical possibilities. The ...c5 advance also supports Black's strategy of undermining our pawn center rather than challenging it head-on with ...e5. Black is now threatening to exchange on d4 or advance to c4, forcing us to make a critical decision about how to maintain our central advantage.

We're advancing our pawn to gain maximum space and close the center, following the most principled approach in the Four Pawns Attack system. This move shuts down Black's ...c5 pawn and creates a fixed pawn structure where we maintain our space advantage while preparing for a kingside attack. The d5 advance also opens the c4-square for potential piece maneuvers and restricts Black's pieces, particularly the light-squared bishop which will struggle to find good squares. By closing the center, we're committing to a kingside attack while Black will typically seek counterplay on the queenside or with pawn breaks like ...f5 or ...e6, leading to the sharp, double-edged positions that characterize this opening system.
